questions.question: Define Turing Machine and list its components.
keyPoints: Turing Machine is a theoretical computing device with infinite tape
Components: Tape (infinite), Read/Write Head, State Register, Transition Function, Finite Control
Formal definition: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, B, F)

questions.question: Explain the difference between decidable and recognizable languages with examples.
keyPoints: Decidable: TM halts on all inputs (always gives yes/no)
Recognizable: TM halts and accepts if string in language, may loop otherwise
Example of decidable: Checking if number is prime
Example of recognizable: Halting problem language

questions.question: State and explain Church-Turing Thesis.
keyPoints: States that any intuitively computable function can be computed by a Turing Machine
Provides formal definition of computability
Hasn't been proven but widely accepted
Connects mathematical and intuitive notions of computation

questions.question: What is a Universal Turing Machine? Why is it important?
keyPoints: UTM can simulate any other Turing Machine
Takes description of any TM and its input as its own input
Important because it established the concept of stored-program computers
Foundation of modern computing - a single machine can perform any computation

questions.question: Explain the Halting Problem and its significance.
keyPoints: Problem: Given a program and input, determine if program halts
Turing proved it undecidable - no algorithm can solve it for all cases
Uses proof by contradiction and self-reference
Significance: Shows there are problems beyond computational limits; has implications for program verification, AI limits

questions.question: Differentiate between Deterministic and Non-Deterministic Turing Machines.
keyPoints: DTM: At most one move per state-symbol pair (single branch)
NDTM: Multiple possible moves per state-symbol (computation tree)
NDTM can be simulated by DTM with exponential time complexity
Both accept the same class of languages (RE)

questions.question: What is the significance of blank symbol B in Turing Machine?
keyPoints: B represents blank cells on tape
Not part of input alphabet Σ
Used to mark end of input and provide working space
Allows TM to overwrite input symbols during computation

questions.question: Provide formal definition of Turing Machine and explain each component with significance.
keyPoints: Formal definition: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, B, F)
Q: Finite set of states - represents 'memory modes'
Σ: Input alphabet (excluding blank) - symbols machine can read
Γ: Tape alphabet (Σ ∪ {B}) - symbols that can be written
δ: Transition function - defines behavior (q,X) → (p,Y,D)
q0: Start state - computation begins here
B: Blank symbol - marks empty tape cells
F: Final/accepting states - causes halting with acceptance
Explain how components work together in computation

questions.question: Explain how Turing Machine can be used to decide a language. Compare with recognition. Give examples of decidable and undecidable problems.
keyPoints: Deciding: TM halts on ALL inputs - always accepts or rejects
Recognizing: TM halts on accepted strings, may loop on rejected
Decidable example: Checking if string is in regular language, parity checking
Undecidable example: Halting problem, Post Correspondence Problem
Emphasize: Decidable ⊂ Recognizable, all decidable are recognizable but not vice versa

questions.question: Design a Turing Machine to accept the language L = {0^n 1^n | n ≥ 1}. Explain the algorithm and trace for input '0011'.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Use 'marking' technique - replace 0 with X moving right, find matching 1 to replace with Y
Process: Move right, mark 0 with X, skip X/Y, find unmarked 1, mark with Y, return left
Continue until all matched or reject if mismatch found
Trace for 0011: q0→X011→X0Y1→XY11→accept
Emphasize use of tape for counting/matching, need to return to find next 0

questions.question: Prove that the Halting Problem is undecidable using Turing Machine concept.
keyPoints: Assume there exists a TM H that decides Halting Problem
H(p, w) = 'yes' if p halts on w, 'no' if p loops on w
Construct new TM D that uses H as subroutine
D takes input (M), runs H on (M, M) - self-reference
If H says M halts on M, D enters infinite loop; if H says loops, D halts
Now run D on its own description D: If D halts → loops, if D loops → halts
Contradiction! Therefore H cannot exist - Halting Problem undecidable
